nodes.title: Measure an angle by the rotation of a ray and assign its sign
nodes.brief: Treat an angle as the amount of rotation of a ray about its initial point, with anticlockwise rotation positive and clockwise rotation negative.
nodes.quote: If the direction of rotation is anticlockwise, the angle is said to be positive and if the direction of rotation is clockwise, then the angle is *negative*
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — An angle is the amount of rotation of a ray, with no upper limit.
The starting ray is the initial side, its final position is the terminal side, and the fixed point is the vertex. The angle measures how much the ray rotates from initial to terminal side. There is no upper limit — angles can exceed 360° or complete multiple full revolutions (e.g. 420°, or negative 720°).

PART 2 — The sign records direction: anticlockwise is positive, clockwise is negative.
Anticlockwise rotation produces a positive angle and clockwise rotation produces a negative angle. For example, −90° (clockwise quarter-turn) has the same terminal side as +270° (anticlockwise three-quarter-turn) but carries a different sign.
keyMoves: Identify the initial side, terminal side and vertex of a rotation
Read the angle as the amount of rotation from initial to terminal side
Assign a positive angle to anticlockwise and a negative angle to clockwise rotation
misconceptions: Thinking an angle cannot exceed 360 deg or be negative
Confusing the initial side with the terminal side when reading the sign

nodes.title: Express an angle in degrees, minutes and seconds
nodes.brief: Use the degree as one 360th of a revolution and subdivide it into minutes and seconds (1 deg = 60', 1' = 60'').
nodes.quote: A degree is divided into 60 minutes, and a minute is divided into 60 seconds.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — One degree is 1/360 of a full revolution; subdivide with 1° = 60′ and 1′ = 60″.
The degree (°) measures angles as fractions of a revolution: 1° = 1/360 of one full revolution. For finer measurement, each degree is divided into 60 minutes (1° = 60′) and each minute into 60 seconds (1′ = 60″). Common angles: 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°.

PART 2 — Convert minutes and seconds to a fractional degree before arithmetic.
The subdivision is base 60 (sexagesimal), not base 10. To combine 40° 20′: 40° 20′ = 40 + 20/60 = 40⅓°. Never add degrees and minutes as if they are the same unit — 40° 20′ ≠ 60°.
keyMoves: State that 1 degree is one 360th of one revolution
Apply 1 deg = 60' and 1' = 60'' to subdivide a degree
Convert minutes and seconds into a fraction of a degree (e.g. 20' = 1/3 deg)
misconceptions: Treating a minute as one 100th of a degree (decimal) instead of one 60th
Adding degrees and minutes as if both were in the same units

nodes.title: Define the radian as the angle subtended by an arc equal to the radius
nodes.brief: Define one radian as the angle an arc of length one unit subtends at the centre of a unit circle, and treat radian measures as real numbers.
nodes.quote: Angle subtended at the centre by an arc of length 1 unit in a unit circle (circle of radius 1 unit) is said to have a measure of 1 radian.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — One radian is the central angle when arc length equals the radius.
In a circle of radius r, an arc of length r subtends exactly 1 radian at the centre. In a unit circle (r = 1), arc length 1 gives 1 radian. The full circumference is 2*pi*r, so a complete revolution subtends 2*pi radians. Therefore pi radian = 180°.

PART 2 — Radian measures are real numbers; the word 'radian' is usually dropped.
By wrapping the real number line around the unit circle, every real number maps to a radian measure. By convention the word 'radian' is omitted: writing 'pi' means pi radians = 180°. Treat radian measures and real numbers as interchangeable.
keyMoves: Define 1 radian via an arc of length 1 in a unit circle (or length r in radius-r circle)
State that one full revolution is 2 pi radian
Treat radian measures and real numbers as interchangeable (drop the word 'radian' by convention)
misconceptions: Thinking a radian is a fixed number of degrees defined arbitrarily rather than via arc-to-radius
Believing radians and real numbers are different kinds of quantity

nodes.title: Relate arc length, radius and central angle by l = r theta
nodes.brief: Use l = r theta (theta in radians) to find any one of arc length, radius or central angle from the other two.
nodes.quote: in a circle of radius r, an arc of length l subtends an angle  $\theta$  radian at the centre
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula l = r*theta with theta in radians.
In a circle of radius r, an arc of length l subtends a central angle theta radians where l = r*theta. This follows from the definition of radian: an arc of length r subtends exactly 1 radian. Rearrange to get theta = l/r or r = l/theta.

PART 2 — Always convert degrees to radians before applying the formula.
The formula l = r*theta requires theta in radians. Multiply degrees by pi/180 first. Example: 60 degrees = 60*(pi/180) = pi/3 radians.

PART 3 — Apply to find any one of l, r, or theta from the other two.
Example: a minute hand of length 1.5 cm sweeps 40 minutes = 240 degrees = 4*pi/3 radians; tip travels l = 1.5 * (4*pi/3) = 2*pi cm.
keyMoves: Convert the central angle to radians before using the formula
Apply l = r theta (or theta = l/r, r = l/theta) to find the unknown quantity
Use equal arcs in two circles to compare radii via l = r1 theta1 = r2 theta2
misconceptions: Using the central angle in degrees in l = r theta without converting to radians
Confusing arc length with chord length

nodes.title: Convert between degree and radian measure
nodes.brief: Use pi radian = 180 deg to convert any angle between degrees and radians.
nodes.quote: a circle subtends at the centre an angle whose radian measure is  $2\pi$  and its degree measure is  $360^{\circ}$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The single relation: pi radians = 180°. Derive both conversion multipliers from it.
A full circle is 360° = 2*pi radians, so pi radians = 180°. From this: radians = degrees * (pi/180), and degrees = radians * (180/pi). Multiply by pi/180 to go degrees → radians; multiply by 180/pi to go radians → degrees.

PART 2 — Memorise the standard angle table and avoid the wrong multiplier direction.
Common conversions: 30° = pi/6, 45° = pi/4, 60° = pi/3, 90° = pi/2, 180° = pi, 270° = 3*pi/2, 360° = 2*pi. The most common error is using 180/pi when converting from degrees (should be pi/180) — remember 'degrees are bigger numbers, divide by 180 to shrink them to radians'.
keyMoves: Start from pi radian = 180 deg
Multiply a degree measure by pi/180 to get radians
Multiply a radian measure by 180/pi to get degrees
misconceptions: Multiplying by 180/pi to go from degrees to radians (wrong direction)
Forgetting to convert minutes/seconds to a fractional degree before applying pi/180

nodes.title: Define cos x and sin x as the coordinates of a unit-circle point
nodes.brief: For a point P(a, b) on the unit circle at angle x, define cos x = a and sin x = b, extending the trig ratios to any angle.
nodes.quote: We define  $\cos x = a$  and  $\sin x = b$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Place a point P on the unit circle at angle x and read off its coordinates.
Centre a circle of radius 1 at the origin. For an angle x (in radians), let P(a, b) be the point on the circle where the angle AOP = x. Define cos x = a (x-coordinate) and sin x = b (y-coordinate). This extends sine and cosine to ALL real values of x, not just acute angles.

PART 2 — Read quadrantal values directly from coordinates.
At key angles: (0°) P=(1,0): cos 0=1, sin 0=0. (90°=pi/2) P=(0,1): cos(pi/2)=0, sin(pi/2)=1. (180°=pi) P=(-1,0): cos pi=-1, sin pi=0. (270°=3pi/2) P=(0,-1): cos(3pi/2)=0, sin(3pi/2)=-1. (360°=2pi) back to (1,0). Do not swap: cos is x-coordinate, sin is y-coordinate.
keyMoves: Place P at angle x on the unit circle centred at the origin
Read cos x as the x-coordinate a and sin x as the y-coordinate b of P
Evaluate sin and cos at quadrantal angles 0, pi/2, pi, 3 pi/2, 2 pi from the coordinates of A, B, C, D
misconceptions: Swapping the coordinates: taking sin x = a and cos x = b
Believing sine and cosine are only defined for acute angles in a triangle

nodes.title: Establish the identity sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1
nodes.brief: Use that P(a, b) lies on the unit circle (a^2 + b^2 = 1) to prove cos^2 x + sin^2 x = 1 for every angle x.
nodes.quote: or  $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The identity comes from Pythagoras on the unit circle.
The point P(a,b) lies on the unit circle, so a^2 + b^2 = 1 (the circle's equation, equivalently Pythagoras on triangle OMP). Since cos x = a and sin x = b, substituting gives cos^2 x + sin^2 x = 1, holding for EVERY real x.

PART 2 — Use it to find one ratio from the other, fixing sign by quadrant.
Given sin x, then cos^2 x = 1 - sin^2 x, so cos x = +/- sqrt(1 - sin^2 x); choose the sign from the quadrant of x. Note sin^2 x means (sin x)^2, NOT sin(x^2), and (sin x + cos x)^2 is not 1. The identity also produces 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x by dividing through by cos^2 x.
keyMoves: Use a^2 + b^2 = 1 because P lies on the unit circle (Pythagoras in triangle OMP)
Substitute cos x = a, sin x = b to get sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1
Solve for the unknown ratio, choosing the sign from the quadrant
misconceptions: Writing (sin x + cos x)^2 = 1 instead of sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1
Forgetting the quadrant when taking the square root to fix the sign

nodes.title: Define cosec, sec, tan and cot from sin and cos
nodes.brief: Define the other four trig functions as cosec x = 1/sin x, sec x = 1/cos x, tan x = sin x/cos x, cot x = cos x/sin x, with their exclusions.
nodes.quote: We now define other trigonometric functions in terms of sine and cosine functions:
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Define the four functions from sin and cos.
cosec x = 1/sin x, sec x = 1/cos x, tan x = sin x/cos x, cot x = cos x/sin x. Watch the pairing: cosec is the reciprocal of SINE (not cosine) and sec is the reciprocal of COSINE (not sine) — the names are crossed relative to what you might guess.

PART 2 — Exclude the angles where the denominator vanishes.
cosec x and cot x are undefined where sin x = 0, i.e. x = n*pi. sec x and tan x are undefined where cos x = 0, i.e. x = (2n+1)*pi/2. At those angles the function does not exist because you would divide by zero.
keyMoves: Write cosec x = 1/sin x and sec x = 1/cos x as reciprocals
Write tan x = sin x/cos x and cot x = cos x/sin x as ratios
Exclude the angles where the denominator is zero (x = n pi or (2n+1) pi/2)
misconceptions: Pairing sec with sin and cosec with cos (the reciprocals are crossed)
Forgetting that tan and sec are undefined at odd multiples of pi/2

nodes.title: Derive 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x and 1 + cot^2 x = cosec^2 x
nodes.brief: Divide sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1 by cos^2 x and by sin^2 x to obtain the two derived Pythagorean identities.
nodes.quote: $$1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x \qquad (\text{why?})$$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Divide the fundamental identity by cos^2 x to get 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x.
Start from sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1. Divide every term by cos^2 x: (sin^2 x/cos^2 x) + 1 = 1/cos^2 x, which is tan^2 x + 1 = sec^2 x. Rearranged: 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x.

PART 2 — Divide by sin^2 x to get 1 + cot^2 x = cosec^2 x, and watch the signs.
Dividing sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1 by sin^2 x gives 1 + cot^2 x = cosec^2 x. Both identities have a PLUS sign: 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x (not 1 - tan^2 x), and do not mix the pairs (tan goes with sec, cot goes with cosec).
keyMoves: Start from sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1
Divide by cos^2 x to get 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x
Divide by sin^2 x to get 1 + cot^2 x = cosec^2 x
misconceptions: Writing 1 - tan^2 x = sec^2 x (wrong sign)
Mixing the pairs, e.g. 1 + tan^2 x = cosec^2 x

nodes.title: Determine the sign of a trig function from its quadrant
nodes.brief: Use the signs of the coordinates a, b in each quadrant to decide the sign of each trigonometric function there.
nodes.quote: in the first quadrant  $(0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}) a$  and *b* are both positive
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Signs of cos x and sin x follow the coordinate signs by quadrant.
Since cos x = a (x-coordinate) and sin x = b (y-coordinate): QI (0 to pi/2) both positive; QII (pi/2 to pi) a negative, b positive; QIII (pi to 3pi/2) both negative; QIV (3pi/2 to 2pi) a positive, b negative. So sin x > 0 in QI and QII; cos x > 0 in QI and QIV.

PART 2 — Use the ASTC rule and derive the other four functions.
A handy summary: All positive in QI, Sine in QII, Tangent in QIII, Cosine in QIV (ASTC). tan, cot, sec, cosec take their signs from sin and cos (e.g. tan = sin/cos is positive when both have the same sign — QI and QIII). This pins down the sign when an identity gives a value only up to +/-.
keyMoves: Identify the quadrant of the angle
Read the signs of a = cos x and b = sin x there
Deduce the sign of tan, cot, sec, cosec from sin and cos
misconceptions: Thinking all functions are positive in every quadrant
Getting the cos sign wrong in the fourth quadrant (cos is positive there)

nodes.title: State the domain and range of each trigonometric function
nodes.brief: Give the domain and range of sin, cos, cosec, sec, tan and cot, including the bound -1 <= sin x, cos x <= 1.
nodes.quote: Thus, domain of  $y = \sin x$  and  $y = \cos x$  is the set of all real numbers and range is the interval [-1, 1]
nodes.explanation: Since the unit-circle coordinates satisfy -1 <= a <= 1 and -1 <= b <= 1, we have -1 <= cos x <= 1 and -1 <= sin x <= 1 for all x. So y = sin x and y = cos x have domain all real numbers and range [-1, 1]. For the reciprocals: cosec x has domain {x : x != n pi} and range {y : y >= 1 or y <= -1}; sec x has domain {x : x != (2n+1) pi/2} and the same range; tan x has domain {x : x != (2n+1) pi/2} and range all reals; cot x has domain {x : x != n pi} and range all reals. The excluded points are where the defining denominator vanishes.
keyMoves: Use -1 <= sin x <= 1 and -1 <= cos x <= 1 to set the range of sin and cos
Exclude n pi for cosec and cot, and (2n+1) pi/2 for sec and tan, from the domain
Give range |y| >= 1 for sec and cosec, and all reals for tan and cot
misconceptions: Claiming sin x or cos x can exceed 1 in magnitude
Giving tan x a bounded range like [-1, 1] instead of all reals

nodes.title: Use the periodicity of trig functions to reduce and sketch them
nodes.brief: Apply sin(2n pi + x) = sin x, cos(2n pi + x) = cos x and tan(pi + x) = tan x to reduce large angles and sketch the graphs.
nodes.quote: if x increases (or decreases) by any integral multiple of  $2\pi$ , the values of sine and cosine functions do not change.
nodes.explanation: Going one full revolution returns P to the same point, so if x changes by any integer multiple of 2 pi the values of sine and cosine are unchanged: sin(2n pi + x) = sin x and cos(2n pi + x) = cos x for n in Z. Hence cosec and sec also repeat after 2 pi, while tan(pi + x) = tan x makes tan and cot repeat after pi. This periodicity is used to evaluate functions of large or negative angles by subtracting full periods (e.g. sin(31 pi/3) = sin(pi/3), cos(-1710 deg) = cos 90 deg = 0), and it dictates the repeating shape of the graphs of these functions.
keyMoves: Reduce a large angle by adding/subtracting multiples of 2 pi for sin, cos, sec, cosec
Reduce by multiples of pi for tan and cot
Use the reduced angle to read off the value or to sketch the repeating graph
misconceptions: Using period 2 pi for tan and cot instead of pi
Forgetting that periodicity also lets negative angles be reduced into a standard range

nodes.title: Find all six trig values from one value and the quadrant
nodes.brief: Given one trig function and the quadrant, use the identities to find the other five, fixing each sign by the quadrant.
nodes.quote: find the values of other five trigonometric functions.
nodes.explanation: Given one trigonometric value and the quadrant of x, the others follow from the identities. For example, if cos x = -3/5 with x in the third quadrant, then sec x = -5/3; sin^2 x = 1 - cos^2 x = 16/25, and since sin is negative in the third quadrant sin x = -4/5, so cosec x = -5/4, tan x = sin x/cos x = 4/3 and cot x = 3/4. The recipe is: use sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 (or 1 + tan^2 = sec^2, etc.) to get a second value up to sign, then fix the sign from the quadrant, then take reciprocals and ratios for the rest.
keyMoves: Use a Pythagorean identity to get a second function up to a sign
Fix the sign using the quadrant of x
Take reciprocals and ratios to obtain the remaining functions
misconceptions: Reporting a value with the wrong sign by ignoring the quadrant
Forgetting that cosec, sec, cot are reciprocals of sin, cos, tan

nodes.title: Apply the even-odd identities cos(-x) = cos x, sin(-x) = -sin x
nodes.brief: Use that reflecting P across the x-axis sends x to -x and b to -b to get cos(-x) = cos x and sin(-x) = -sin x.
nodes.quote: $\cos\left(-x\right) = \cos x$
nodes.explanation: If P(a, b) is the point at angle x on the unit circle, then the point Q at angle -x is the reflection (a, -b) across the x-axis. Reading off coordinates gives cos(-x) = a = cos x (cosine is even) and sin(-x) = -b = -sin x (sine is odd). These even-odd properties extend to the other functions (e.g. tan(-x) = -tan x) and are used constantly to simplify expressions involving negative angles before applying the sum and difference formulas.
keyMoves: Reflect P across the x-axis to get the point at angle -x as (a, -b)
Conclude cos(-x) = cos x (even)
Conclude sin(-x) = -sin x (odd)
misconceptions: Writing cos(-x) = -cos x (cosine is even, not odd)
Writing sin(-x) = sin x (sine is odd, not even)

nodes.title: Prove and apply cos(x +/- y)
nodes.brief: Establish cos(x + y) = cos x cos y - sin x sin y and cos(x - y) = cos x cos y + sin x sin y, then use them to evaluate angles.
nodes.quote: $\cos (x + y) = \cos x \cos y - \sin x \sin y$
nodes.explanation: Using the unit circle and the distance formula on congruent chords P1P3 and P2P4, one proves the cornerstone identity cos(x + y) = cos x cos y - sin x sin y. Replacing y by -y (and using the even-odd identities) gives cos(x - y) = cos x cos y + sin x sin y. These let you evaluate cosines of non-standard angles by splitting them into standard ones (e.g. cos 15 deg = cos(45 deg - 30 deg)). They are the seed identities from which the sine sum/difference, the cofunction, tan and cot sum formulas, and the multiple-angle formulas are all derived.
keyMoves: Prove cos(x + y) = cos x cos y - sin x sin y from congruent chords on the unit circle
Replace y by -y to get cos(x - y) = cos x cos y + sin x sin y
Split a target angle into a sum/difference of standard angles to evaluate its cosine
misconceptions: Writing cos(x + y) = cos x cos y + sin x sin y (wrong sign)
Distributing as cos(x + y) = cos x + cos y

nodes.title: Derive the cofunction and allied-angle identities
nodes.brief: Use the cos(x - y) formula to get cos(pi/2 - x) = sin x, sin(pi/2 - x) = cos x and the related allied-angle reductions.
nodes.quote: 5.  $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) = \sin x$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Derive the basic cofunction identities from cos(x-y).
Substitute x=pi/2 and y=x in cos(x-y): cos(pi/2-x) = cos(pi/2)cos x + sin(pi/2)sin x = 0*cos x + 1*sin x = sin x. Then sin(pi/2-x) = cos(pi/2-(pi/2-x)) = cos x.

PART 2 — Allied-angle table for pi/2+x, pi-x, pi+x and 2pi-x.
cos(pi/2+x)=-sin x; sin(pi/2+x)=cos x; cos(pi-x)=-cos x; sin(pi-x)=sin x; cos(pi+x)=-cos x; sin(pi+x)=-sin x; cos(2pi-x)=cos x; sin(2pi-x)=-sin x.

PART 3 — Pattern: function swaps at pi/2 offsets; sign depends on quadrant.
At pi/2 +/- x: cos and sin interchange (cofunction). At pi +/- x: the function name stays the same but sign is determined by quadrant. Use this pattern to reduce any angle rather than memorising all eight identities separately.
keyMoves: Substitute x = pi/2, y = x in cos(x - y) to get cos(pi/2 - x) = sin x
Deduce sin(pi/2 - x) = cos x from the cofunction relation
Apply allied-angle reductions for pi/2 +/- x, pi +/- x, 2 pi - x
misconceptions: Writing cos(pi/2 + x) = sin x instead of -sin x
Forgetting the sign change in sin(pi + x) = -sin x

nodes.title: Prove and apply sin(x +/- y)
nodes.brief: Establish sin(x + y) = sin x cos y + cos x sin y and sin(x - y) = sin x cos y - cos x sin y, then use them to evaluate angles.
nodes.quote: $\sin (x + y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y$
nodes.explanation: Writing sin(x + y) = cos(pi/2 - (x + y)) = cos((pi/2 - x) - y) and applying the cos(x - y) formula gives sin(x + y) = sin x cos y + cos x sin y. Replacing y by -y yields sin(x - y) = sin x cos y - cos x sin y. Like their cosine analogues, these evaluate sines of non-standard angles by decomposition (e.g. sin 15 deg = sin(45 deg - 30 deg) = (sqrt(3) - 1)/(2 sqrt(2))) and feed the double- and triple-angle and sum-to-product identities.
keyMoves: Use the cofunction identity to write sin(x + y) as a cosine of a difference
Apply cos(x - y) to obtain sin(x + y) = sin x cos y + cos x sin y
Replace y by -y to get sin(x - y) = sin x cos y - cos x sin y
misconceptions: Writing sin(x + y) = sin x cos y - cos x sin y (wrong sign for the sum)
Distributing as sin(x + y) = sin x + sin y

nodes.title: Derive and apply tan(x +/- y) and cot(x +/- y)
nodes.brief: Obtain tan(x +/- y) and cot(x +/- y) by dividing the sine and cosine sum/difference formulas, with the stated angle restrictions.
nodes.quote: $$\tan (x + y) = \frac{\tan x + \tan y}{1 - \tan x \tan y}$$
nodes.explanation: Provided none of x, y, (x + y) is an odd multiple of pi/2, dividing sin(x + y) by cos(x + y) and then numerator and denominator by cos x cos y gives tan(x + y) = (tan x + tan y)/(1 - tan x tan y); replacing y by -y gives tan(x - y) = (tan x - tan y)/(1 + tan x tan y). Similarly, when none of x, y, (x + y) is a multiple of pi, dividing by sin x sin y gives cot(x + y) = (cot x cot y - 1)/(cot y + cot x), and cot(x - y) = (cot x cot y + 1)/(cot y - cot x). These are used to evaluate tangents of compound angles (e.g. tan(13 pi/12)) and to prove identities such as tan 3x tan 2x tan x = tan 3x - tan 2x - tan x.
keyMoves: Divide sin(x + y) by cos(x + y), then by cos x cos y, to get the tan formula
Replace y by -y for tan(x - y); divide by sin x sin y for the cot formulas
Respect the restrictions (no odd multiple of pi/2 for tan; no multiple of pi for cot)
misconceptions: Writing tan(x + y) with a plus sign in the denominator
Ignoring the excluded angles where the formula is undefined

nodes.title: Derive and apply the double-angle identities
nodes.brief: Put y = x in the sum formulas to get cos 2x, sin 2x and tan 2x in their several equivalent forms.
nodes.quote: $$\cos 2x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x = 2 \cos^2 x - 1 = 1 - 2 \sin^2 x = \frac{1 - \tan^2 x}{1 + \tan^2 x}$$
nodes.explanation: Setting y = x in the sum formulas gives the double-angle identities. cos 2x = cos^2 x - sin^2 x = 2 cos^2 x - 1 = 1 - 2 sin^2 x = (1 - tan^2 x)/(1 + tan^2 x). sin 2x = 2 sin x cos x = 2 tan x/(1 + tan^2 x). tan 2x = 2 tan x/(1 - tan^2 x) (for 2x not an odd multiple of pi/2). The several forms of cos 2x are interchangeable via sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 and are the ones used to convert between sin^2 x, cos^2 x and cos 2x (e.g. cos^2 x = (1 + cos 2x)/2), which underlies many proofs and the half-angle results.
keyMoves: Put y = x in cos(x + y), sin(x + y), tan(x + y) to get the double-angle forms
Use sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 to switch between the three forms of cos 2x
Apply 2 sin^2 x = 1 - cos 2x and 2 cos^2 x = 1 + cos 2x to reduce squared functions
misconceptions: Writing sin 2x = 2 sin x or cos 2x = 2 cos x
Using the wrong sign form of cos 2x for the quantity you actually need

nodes.title: Derive and apply the triple-angle identities
nodes.brief: Use sin 3x = sin(2x + x) etc. to obtain sin 3x, cos 3x and tan 3x in terms of the single angle.
nodes.quote: $\sin 3x = 3 \sin x - 4 \sin^3 x$
nodes.explanation: Writing 3x = 2x + x and applying the sum and double-angle formulas gives the triple-angle identities. sin 3x = 3 sin x - 4 sin^3 x; cos 3x = 4 cos^3 x - 3 cos x; tan 3x = (3 tan x - tan^3 x)/(1 - 3 tan^2 x) (for 3x not an odd multiple of pi/2). These express a function of 3x purely in terms of the single angle x and are used both to evaluate and to prove identities involving tripled angles.
keyMoves: Write 3x = 2x + x and apply the sum formula
Substitute the double-angle forms of sin 2x, cos 2x (or tan 2x)
Simplify to sin 3x = 3 sin x - 4 sin^3 x, cos 3x = 4 cos^3 x - 3 cos x, tan 3x form
misconceptions: Writing sin 3x = 3 sin x (linear, dropping the cubic term)
Swapping the coefficients between the sin 3x and cos 3x formulas

nodes.title: Apply the sum-to-product identities
nodes.brief: Use cos x +/- cos y and sin x +/- sin y written as products of cosines/sines of (x+y)/2 and (x-y)/2.
nodes.quote: (i)  $\cos x + \cos y = 2\cos \frac{x+y}{2}\cos \frac{x-y}{2}$
nodes.explanation: Adding and subtracting the cos(x + y), cos(x - y) and sin(x + y), sin(x - y) formulas, then substituting x = (theta + phi)/2, y = (theta - phi)/2, gives the sum-to-product formulas: cos x + cos y = 2 cos((x+y)/2) cos((x-y)/2); cos x - cos y = -2 sin((x+y)/2) sin((x-y)/2); sin x + sin y = 2 sin((x+y)/2) cos((x-y)/2); sin x - sin y = 2 cos((x+y)/2) sin((x-y)/2). They turn a sum or difference of two sines or cosines into a product, which is the key step in simplifying ratios like (cos 7x + cos 5x)/(sin 7x - sin 5x) = cot x.
keyMoves: Identify whether the expression is a sum/difference of two cosines or two sines
Apply the matching formula with half-sum (x+y)/2 and half-difference (x-y)/2
Cancel common product factors to simplify the resulting expression
misconceptions: Dropping the minus sign in cos x - cos y = -2 sin(...) sin(...)
Swapping (x+y)/2 and (x-y)/2 between the two factors

nodes.title: Apply the product-to-sum identities
nodes.brief: Use 2 cos x cos y, -2 sin x sin y, 2 sin x cos y and 2 cos x sin y written as sums/differences of cosines and sines.
nodes.quote: As a part of identities given in 20, we can prove the following results:
nodes.explanation: Reversing the additions used for the sum-to-product formulas gives the product-to-sum identities: 2 cos x cos y = cos(x + y) + cos(x - y); -2 sin x sin y = cos(x + y) - cos(x - y); 2 sin x cos y = sin(x + y) + sin(x - y); 2 cos x sin y = sin(x + y) - sin(x - y). They convert a product of two trig functions into a sum or difference, which linearises products inside proofs and integrals (e.g. expanding 2 cos 2x cos(x/2) before combining terms).
keyMoves: Recognise a product of two sines/cosines to be linearised
Apply the matching product-to-sum formula to write it as a sum or difference
Combine the resulting cosines/sines with other terms to simplify
misconceptions: Forgetting the leading factor of 2 (or the minus sign in -2 sin x sin y)
Confusing 2 sin x cos y with 2 cos x sin y (different difference terms)

nodes.title: Prove and apply trigonometric identities to evaluate and simplify
nodes.brief: Combine the unit-circle definitions, the Pythagorean identities and the sum/difference, multiple-angle and product-sum formulas to prove identities and evaluate trigonometric expressions.
nodes.quote: The basic results in this connection are called *trigonometric identities*.
nodes.explanation: The chapter generalises the trigonometric ratios to functions of any real angle and builds the full toolkit of trigonometric identities. Everything rests on cos x and sin x as unit-circle coordinates, the fundamental identity sin^2 x + cos^2 x = 1 with its derived forms, and the cos(x +/- y) seed from which the sine, tangent, cotangent, cofunction, double-angle, triple-angle, sum-to-product and product-to-sum identities all follow. The overall skill is to choose and combine these identities - reducing angles by periodicity and allied-angle rules, fixing signs by quadrant - to evaluate non-standard angles and to prove or simplify trigonometric expressions.
keyMoves: Select the identities that match the structure of the expression
Reduce angles using periodicity, even-odd and allied-angle rules, fixing signs by quadrant
Chain sum/difference, multiple-angle and product-sum identities to prove or evaluate
misconceptions: Treating trig functions as linear (e.g. sin(x + y) = sin x + sin y)
Applying an identity outside its stated angle restrictions or with the wrong sign
